skill.name: wp-docker
skill.description: Set up and operate a local Docker Compose WordPress development stack with WP-CLI automation, plugin installation, and site setup scripts.
skill.summary: Build a local WordPress Docker development environment with WP-CLI setup workflows.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and environment patterns. Review confirms the skill is a legitimate WordPress Docker helper, but it runs Docker and WP-CLI commands, downloads plugins, and includes weak local-development default credentials that require user caution.

medium_findings.title: Weak Default Credentials in Development Stack
medium_findings.description: The Docker Compose template and setup script include predictable default database and WordPress administrator passwords. This is acceptable only for isolated local development and becomes dangerous if reused or exposed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The defaults are visible in the template and setup script, and the quick-start example also uses a simple password. The risk depends on deployment context, so this is high confidence but medium severity.

medium_findings.title: Docker and WP-CLI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run docker-compose and WP-CLI commands that create containers, edit WordPress data, import databases, and remove volumes. These actions are legitimate for the skill but can change local files and containers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Docker and WP-CLI workflows rather than hidden execution. They are still impactful because they can create, modify, or destroy local development state.

medium_findings.title: Network Plugin Installation During Setup
medium_findings.description: The setup workflow installs WordPress plugins by slug, which normally downloads packages from the WordPress plugin ecosystem. This is expected behavior, but users should review plugin choices and supply-chain risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The plugin installation commands are directly present and semantically tied to the setup workflow. No malicious endpoint is shown, but the network and supply-chain behavior is real.

medium_findings.title: Administrative Password Printed to Terminal
medium_findings.description: The setup script prints the WordPress administrator password at the end of execution. This can leak credentials through terminal history, logs, screen sharing, or copied output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly echoes the password variable in the completion output. The exposure is local, but it is a clear credential-handling weakness.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Triggered Static Shell Alerts
low_findings.description: Most SKILL.md command findings are fenced markdown examples for Docker and WP-CLI usage. They are not hidden Ruby backtick execution, but the skill may ask an agent to run similar commands when requested.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows markdown code fences and documented commands, not Ruby code execution. The finding is mostly a scanner false positive with legitimate operational context.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Template Path Is Documentation
low_findings.description: The references to ~/.claude describe where templates are stored and copied from. This is filesystem access guidance, not evidence of secret file harvesting or hidden-file exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path appears only as the skill template location. No evidence found of reading shell profiles, keys, tokens, or unrelated hidden files.

low_findings.title: Email Findings Are WordPress Admin Configuration
low_findings.description: The email-related static hits are administrator email values and a mail plugin slug. The setup command uses --skip-email, so no direct email sending behavior is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines configure an admin email and install wp-mail-smtp. No evidence found that the script sends email or exfiltrates data through email.

low_findings.title: LLM Role Token and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The docker-compose findings are WordPress environment variable names, and the SKILL.md weak-crypto alerts match ordinary markdown text rather than cryptographic code. No prompt injection or weak cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited docker-compose lines contain environment keys and defaults, not chat role markers. Review found no instruction text attempting to override the audit or impersonate system messages.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Volume Removal Command Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes docker-compose down -v, which removes persistent database volumes. It is clearly labeled destructive, but users should confirm before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destructive command is explicitly present and labeled. It is not malicious, but it can delete local database state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Import and Search-Replace Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents database import and search-replace commands for migrations. These operations can overwrite data or alter many records when used with the wrong files or domains.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The operations are normal WordPress maintenance tasks, but they have broad data impact. The risk comes from operator error rather than hidden behavior.

content.value_statement: WordPress setup often takes repeated Docker, database, plugin, and WP-CLI work. This skill provides templates and workflows for local WordPress development environments.
actual_capabilities: Creates a Docker Compose stack with MariaDB, WordPress, and WP-CLI services.
Provides a shell setup script for WordPress installation and initial configuration.
Documents WP-CLI commands for plugins, themes, pages, options, and database tasks.
Includes PHP upload and memory settings through an uploads.ini template.
Supports migration workflows with database export, import, and URL replacement.
Documents troubleshooting commands for containers, logs, permissions, and database checks.
limitations: Designed for local development, not hardened production hosting.
Uses default credentials unless the user supplies stronger values.
Requires Docker Compose, shell access, and network access for plugin installation.
Does not validate plugin safety or license compatibility before installation.

use_cases.title: Launch a Local WordPress Sandbox
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable Docker environment for theme, plugin, and content work on a local machine.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Client Site Starter
use_cases.description: Install WordPress, activate common plugins, set permalinks, and create starter pages for early build work.

use_cases.title: Move Development Content Between Sites
use_cases.description: Use WP-CLI database export, import, cache flush, and URL replacement workflows during development migrations.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New Local Site
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-docker skill to create a local WordPress development environment for a site named Acme Studio on port 8080.

prompt_templates.title: Customize Setup Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-docker to set up WordPress with my site URL, title, admin username, strong password, and admin email. Explain each command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Audit the Development Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my wp-docker project files and identify unsafe defaults, exposed ports, volume mounts, and plugin installation risks before I run setup.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Development Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-docker to plan a migration from an existing WordPress development site into this Docker environment, including backup, import, URL replacement, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Set up a new local WordPress site for a portfolio project.
output: A Docker Compose stack is prepared for WordPress, MariaDB, and WP-CLI.
The setup plan includes site URL, title, administrator account, plugin installation, and starter pages.
The response warns to replace default passwords before running the setup script.

output_examples.input: Check why my WordPress container cannot connect to the database.
output: The response reviews container status, database health checks, environment values, and Docker logs.
It suggests checking database credentials and waiting for the MariaDB health check before rerunning WP-CLI.

output_examples.input: Prepare a safe development migration workflow.
output: The response outlines backup, database import, URL replacement, cache flush, and verification steps.
It highlights destructive commands and recommends confirming backups before changing database content.

best_practices: Replace all default database and administrator passwords before running setup.
Review Docker commands before execution, especially database import and volume removal commands.
Use this stack on a local or isolated development network unless it is separately hardened.
anti_patterns: Do not use the included default credentials on any shared, public, or production system.
Do not run destructive Docker or database commands without a verified backup.
Do not install the recommended plugins blindly in regulated or security-sensitive environments.

faq.question: Is this skill intended for production WordPress hosting?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for local development and needs hardening before any production use.

faq.question: Does it require Docker?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow depends on Docker Compose services for WordPress, MariaDB, and WP-CLI.

faq.question: Can I choose my own admin password?
faq.answer: Yes. You should provide a strong password instead of using the documented examples or defaults.

faq.question: Does it install plugins automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The setup workflow installs a predefined plugin list through WP-CLI when you run it.

faq.question: Can it help with site migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents WP-CLI database export, import, search-replace, and cache flush workflows.

faq.question: What should I review before running commands?
faq.answer: Review credentials, port exposure, plugin choices, volume mounts, and any command that changes database content.
